eli3: Causal inference is the science of estimating the effect of an intervention (cause) on an outcome, not just predicting their association. Correlation ≠ causation: countries with more TVs have lower infant mortality, but buying TVs won't save babies (wealth is the common cause). Causal inference methods distinguish the causal effect of X on Y from the confounded observed association.

The potential outcomes framework (Rubin) and structural causal models (Pearl's DAGs) are the two main formalisms. Both answer: E[Y(1) − Y(0)] = ATE (Average Treatment Effect) — the average difference in outcome if everyone received treatment vs if everyone received control. The challenge is fundamental: we only observe one potential outcome per unit (the one that actually occurred).

When RCTs are infeasible, quasi-experimental methods estimate causal effects from observational data by exploiting specific sources of variation that mimic randomization: instrumental variables (IV) use an exogenous shock, difference-in-differences (DiD) uses panel data with a treatment timing discontinuity, regression discontinuity (RDD) exploits a cutoff rule, and propensity score matching adjusts for measured confounders.

whenWhy.title: Estimating policy effects from observational data
whenWhy.body: Government policies cannot be randomly assigned — we can't randomly assign people to minimum wage levels, education policies, or tax rates. DiD, RDD, and IV use policy variation as natural experiments. Example: Card & Krueger (1994) used DiD with New Jersey's minimum wage increase (treatment) vs Pennsylvania (control) to estimate the effect on employment, finding no negative effect — contradicting standard economic theory.

whenWhy.title: Difference-in-differences: exploiting policy timing
whenWhy.body: DiD: compare the change in outcome for the treated group (before and after treatment) to the change for the control group (same period). DiD estimator = (ȳ_treated_post − ȳ_treated_pre) − (ȳ_control_post − ȳ_control_pre). Identifying assumption: parallel trends — treated and control groups would have followed the same trend in the absence of treatment. Test: compare pre-treatment trends; placebo tests using pre-treatment periods as 'fake' treatments.

whenWhy.title: Instrumental variables: exploiting exogenous variation
whenWhy.body: IV estimation: find an instrument Z that (1) affects X (relevance: Cov(Z,X) ≠ 0), (2) affects Y only through X (exclusion restriction), (3) is independent of unmeasured confounders. IV estimator: β_IV = Cov(Z,Y)/Cov(Z,X) = (reduced form)/(first stage). Estimates LATE (Local ATE) — the effect for compliers (those whose treatment status changes with Z). Classic instruments: draft lottery for military service, geographic distance to college for education effect on wages.

whenWhy.title: Regression discontinuity: exploiting cutoff rules
whenWhy.body: RDD: when treatment is assigned based on whether a running variable R exceeds a threshold c (e.g., scholarship for test score ≥ 70), compare outcomes just above and below the cutoff. The ATE at the threshold: τ = lim_{r↓c} E[Y|R=r] − lim_{r↑c} E[Y|R=r]. Identification: individuals just above and below c are similar (local randomization near c). Sharp RDD: deterministic assignment. Fuzzy RDD: probabilistic — use RDD as first stage in IV.

deepDiveIntro: Potential outcomes notation: for each unit i and treatment value t, Yᵢ(t) is the outcome if i were assigned to t. Observed outcome: Yᵢ = Yᵢ(Zᵢ). ATE = E[Y(1)−Y(0)]; ATT (average treatment effect on the treated) = E[Y(1)−Y(0)|Z=1]. Under unconfoundedness (Yᵢ(0),Yᵢ(1)) ⊥ Zᵢ|Xᵢ (all confounders X measured), ATE is identified by regression adjustment or propensity score methods.

SUTVA (Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption): (1) no interference — Yᵢ(z₁,…,zₙ) = Yᵢ(zᵢ) (unit i's outcome depends only on its own treatment); (2) consistency — the potential outcome under the observed treatment equals the observed outcome. Both are often violated in practice (network effects violate (1); treatment variation violates (2)).

deepDive.heading: Difference-in-Differences
deepDive.body: DiD model: Yᵢₜ = α + β₁Treatᵢ + β₂Postₜ + τ(Treatᵢ × Postₜ) + εᵢₜ. τ is the DiD estimator of the ATE. Parallel trends assumption: E[Y(0)ᵢₜ − Y(0)ᵢ,ₜ₋₁ | Treatᵢ = 1] = E[Y(0)ᵢₜ − Y(0)ᵢ,ₜ₋₁ | Treatᵢ = 0] — the counterfactual trend for the treated group equals the observed trend for the control. Recent work (Callaway & Sant'Anna 2021, Goodman-Bacon 2021): staggered treatment timing (different units treated at different times) invalidates the standard two-way FE estimator. [1]

deepDive.heading: Instrumental Variables (2SLS)
deepDive.body: Two-Stage Least Squares (2SLS): Stage 1: regress X on Z and controls W to get X̂. Stage 2: regress Y on X̂ and W. 2SLS estimate β_IV = (Z'MX)⁻¹Z'My (where M projects out W). Interpretation: LATE = E[Y(1)−Y(0) | compliers]. Weak instruments (F < 10 in first stage) cause severe small-sample bias toward OLS. Test: Cragg-Donald/Kleibergen-Paap F-statistic for first stage strength. Over-identification test (Sargan-Hansen) checks exclusion restriction when multiple instruments are used. [1]
deepDive.formula: β_IV = Cov(Z, Y) / Cov(Z, X)  [LATE for compliers]

deepDive.heading: Regression Discontinuity Design
deepDive.body: Sharp RDD: τ = E[Y(1)−Y(0)|R=c] = lim_{r↓c}E[Y|R=r] − lim_{r↑c}E[Y|R=r]. Estimated via local linear regression on each side of the cutoff with bandwidth h (chosen via cross-validation or MSE-optimal bandwidth: Imbens & Kalyanaraman 2012). Kernel weighting (triangular kernel preferred) down-weights observations far from c. Validity tests: (1) McCrary density test — no bunching at cutoff; (2) covariate continuity at cutoff; (3) placebo outcomes. [1]

deepDive.heading: Propensity Score Matching
deepDive.body: Under unconfoundedness (Y(0),Y(1)) ⊥ Z|X: ATE identified as E_X[E[Y|Z=1,X] − E[Y|Z=0,X]]. Propensity score e(X) = P(Z=1|X) is a balancing score — conditioning on e(X) blocks all backdoor paths through X. Matching: pair each treated unit with one or more control units with similar e(X). ATT = (1/n₁)Σᵢ:Zᵢ=1 [Yᵢ − Ŷᵢ(0)] where Ŷᵢ(0) is the matched control outcome. Doubly robust estimators (AIPW) are consistent if either the propensity score or outcome model is correct. [1]

takeaways: ATE = E[Y(1)−Y(0)]; identified under unconfoundedness by regression adjustment or propensity score. [1]
DiD: τ = (ȳ_treat,post−ȳ_treat,pre) − (ȳ_ctrl,post−ȳ_ctrl,pre); requires parallel trends assumption. [1]
IV: β_IV = Cov(Z,Y)/Cov(Z,X); estimates LATE for compliers; weak instruments bias toward OLS. [1]
RDD: compares outcomes just above/below assignment threshold; local linear regression with optimal bandwidth. [1]
Doubly robust (AIPW): consistent if either propensity score or outcome model is correctly specified. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between ATE, ATT, and LATE?
edgeCases.a: ATE = E[Y(1)−Y(0)]: average over the full population. ATT = E[Y(1)−Y(0)|Z=1]: average over the treated. ATT is what matters if the treatment would only be applied to similar people as currently treated (replicating a program). LATE = E[Y(1)−Y(0)|complier]: IV estimates the effect only for those whose treatment status responds to the instrument — not necessarily the same as ATE or ATT. These estimands can differ substantially when treatment effects are heterogeneous. [1]

edgeCases.q: When does the parallel trends assumption fail in DiD?
edgeCases.a: Parallel trends fails when: (1) the treatment is endogenous — treated units were already on a different trend pre-treatment (Ashenfelter's dip); (2) there are spillovers — control units are affected by the treatment (SUTVA violation); (3) the composition of groups changes over time (attrition, migration); (4) anticipation effects — treated units change behavior before treatment. Pre-treatment trend tests (event studies) check parallel trends before treatment but cannot test post-treatment counterfactual. Synthetic control (Abadie et al.) constructs a weighted control that matches the treated pre-trends. [1]

sources.label: Angrist & Pischke — Mostly Harmless Econometrics, Ch. 3–6
Pearl — Causality: Models, Reasoning, and Inference (2nd ed.)
